title: German apple cake
subtitle: Versunkener Apfelkuchen — apple-fanned sunken cake
excerpt: A dense, buttery German cake with a layer of thinly sliced apples fanned across the top, pressed in slightly so they sink into the batter. The apples soften and caramelise at the edges during baking. Dust with icing sugar and eat at room temperature.
sourceNotes: The Versunkener Apfelkuchen (sunken apple cake) is a documented German household cake, with recipes appearing in German domestic cookbooks from the nineteenth century, many available through the Internet Archive. The technique of pressing raw sliced fruit into a thick cake batter, which rises around and below the fruit during baking, is documented across German and Austrian domestic cooking manuals. The name refers to the way the apples appear to sink into the surface as the batter rises. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: apple cakes with fruit pressed into the batter are documented in the chapter on fruit cakes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake can be made 24 hours ahead. Keep at room temperature covered loosely. Dust with icing sugar before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The apple moisture keeps the bake gentle — check with a skewer at 40 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A method of decorating a cake where raw sliced fruit is fanned or arranged on top of an unbaked batter and pressed in lightly. As the batter bakes and rises around the fruit, the fruit appears to sink into the surface. The fruit releases juice as it bakes, caramelising at the edges and moistening the crumb beneath.

content.text: This is the simplest form of apple cake: a dense, butter-rich batter spread in a springform tin and topped with apples peeled, cored, and cut into thin slices or fans. Press the apples in lightly — they will appear to sink as the batter rises around them in the oven. The result is a cake with a striking top and a moist, apple-flavoured crumb. Bramley apples give a sharper result; eating apples are milder and hold their shape better.

items.prepNote: medium, peeled, cored, and thinly sliced

content.text: Preheat the oven to 175°C fan. Butter the springform tin and line the base. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} with the {{caster-sugar}} for the batter until pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time, then the {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} until just smooth. Spread the batter evenly into the tin.

Peel, core, and halve the {{apple-eating}}s. Cut each half into thin slices, keeping the half intact. Fan each half out and lay it on the batter surface in a ring, pressing each lightly into the batter. This is the

: the batter will rise around the apples as it bakes. Mix the {{caster-sugar}} for the topping with the {{cinnamon-ground}} and scatter over the apples. Bake for 45–48 minutes until the batter is set around the apples and a skewer inserted into the batter (not an apple) comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes before releasing the springform. Dust with {{icing-sugar}} before serving.
